name: Chris Van Hollen
subject: Dr. King's legacy
disclaimer_text: Paid for by Van Hollen for Senate
body:
"Human progress is neither automatic nor inevitable...Every step toward justice requires sacrifice, suffering, and struggle."
__
Today, I joined celebrations of the life and legacy of Dr. King in different parts of the great state of Maryland, including with the NAACP of Charles County, the United Negro College Fund (UNCF) in Prince Georges County, and Montgomery County's celebration of the "Women of the Dream." Everywhere I went, the people of Maryland stood in solidarity with the millions of Americans who are needlessly suffering because of the shameless government shutdown and called for an end to the madness. About six weeks ago, the man in the White House said he would be "proud" to shutdown the government if he didn't get his way. Mr. President, damaging our country with the longest shutdown in American history is nothing to be proud of!

We are going through a period where the character of our country is being tested. Dr. King dedicated his life to tearing down the walls that separate us in order to build a more perfect union. We know that many today seek instead to divide us and drag us backwards. Dr. King warned about times like these, saying "human progress is neither automatic nor inevitable ... Every step toward justice requires sacrifice, suffering, and struggle." We must rededicate ourselves to that struggle to hold America to her promise of equal rights, equal justice, and equal opportunity.

There is no denying that we have traveled a good distance down the road toward a more equitable society. I will never forget the beautiful sight of Virginia McLaurin, the 106-year-old African American woman--who had lived through Jim Crow, women's suffrage and the civil rights movement--at the dance party at the White House with President Obama and First Lady Michelle Obama.

There is also no denying that there are forces that want to stop our forward progress and reverse the gains we have made. We must not allow that to happen. We must each do everything in our power to bend the arc of the moral universe faster and further toward justice. Remember the powerful words of Dr. King: "The ultimate tragedy is not the oppression and cruelty by the bad people, but the silence over that by the good people." He observed that progress only comes through the "tireless exertions and passionate concern of dedicated individuals." Let us exert ourselves!

Onwards,

Chris

Paid for by Van Hollen for Senate

name: Team Fair Fight
subject: How to truly honor the legacy of Dr. King
disclaimer_text: Paid for by Fair Fight Action
body:
Voting rights is the civil rights battle of our time |
---
[](https://secure.staceyabrams.com/page/m/41efa678/34a064cc/4b72032/70c6175/1066621006/VEsH/ "https://www.fairfightgeorgia.com/?utm_medium=email&utm_source=abrams&utm_content=1&utm_campaign=IE190114Inaug&source=IE190114Inaug")

| | | Friend, Today, the legacy of Martin Luther King Jr. is commemorated around the country with acts of community service. But here in Georgia, in the Deep South that is the cradle of the civil rights movement and the battle for justice, we know that Dr. King's legacy cannot truly be realized in one day alone - not today, not tomorrow, nor even the next day. We are - as we have always been - on the front lines of the struggle for justice at a critical turning point in the struggle for civil rights. And there can be no greater measurement of how far we have come and how far we have to go than the distance between marginalized communities and the ballot box. Voter registrations that are randomly 'put on hold,' absentee ballot requests that are 'misplaced' or thrown out over technicalities, insufficient resources for efficient casting of ballots and barrier after barrier erected to dissuade, prevent, and discourage communities of color from voting - these efforts are the poll taxes of our time. And they are as effective as ever at blocking our power and blunting our force. That is why we carry on Dr. King's legacy not just through words, but through actions. Not just through the singing of praises and the quoting of speeches, but through the grassroots, through the legislature, and through the courts. Through the uncomfortable truths and the harsh realities that cement us to this place of inequality, of injustice, of restriction and denial of our fundamental rights - we fight. Year after year since his death, our country has celebrated Dr. King's words, his message, his struggle, and his service. This year, Fair Fight takes up that mantle, and we join so many others in the hard work, the painful fight, and the difficult struggle to achieve what has been so long denied to so many Americans - the freedom to participate in free and fair elections. Because the greatest honor we can give those who have come before us - those who died for this cause and those who fought in this struggle - is a vow, and a promise, to march on. And together, we will not only make progress, we will win. For our families, for our communities, and for our collective future. That victory will not come today, and it will not come tomorrow. But in the days and weeks and months ahead, one step and one victory at a time, we will get there, and we will win. We were made for such a time as this. Team Fair Fight

name: Tim Kaine
subject: A message from U.S. Coast Guard families
disclaimer_text: Paid for by Kaine for Virginia
body:
They wrote to me because they're hurting, they're worried, and they need someone to fight for them in Washington, D.C.
| | Derek:

Last week, 42,000 members of the U.S. Coast Guard missed their paychecks. The Coast Guard is the only branch of military that is working without pay during this government shutdown. They're responsible for things like port security, search and rescue, defense readiness, and drug interdiction. That's right -- the Coast Guard are the women and men who serve as the first line of defense against illegal drugs coming into our country. And President Trump and Mitch McConnell are refusing to give them their paychecks.

I could write 10 more paragraphs about how mad that makes me. But I'm not going to. Instead, I'm going to share with you the words of these workers, their family, and friends who are feeling the impact of this shutdown every single day. **Take a look at what they have to say:**
> I am directly affected by this senseless government shutdown. I retired from the U.S. Coast Guard in 2006. Since the U.S. Coast Guard is an organization of the Department of Homeland Security, all U.S. Coast Guard personnel (active duty, civilian employees, reservists, and retirees) are being inflicted with undue financial hardship and stress. Today, I went to my local NFCU branch office to enroll in a 0% APR Government Shutdown Assistance Program. Admittedly, I was embarrassed and saddened to have to take this action to maintain some sense of personal financial health and security. My heart goes out to my brothers and sisters of the USCG that are currently on active duty, working without pay, and supporting a family. This is our reality today. Unbelievable!
> --_Katherine, Fairfax County_
>
>
> * * *
>
> My husband works for Coast Guard and is required to work without pay. We suffered a house flood during Hurricane Matthew that wiped out our savings and have a daughter in college. Not sure how we can manage if we miss more than one paycheck as we also assist my mother who has had a stroke. Praying that this is over sooner than later.
> --_Lisa, Ashburn_
>
>
> * * *
>
> My Coast Guard son and his family living in Kodiak, Alaska are not getting paid. Risking his life as a rescue pilot with no pay is unpatriotic as well as dangerous for the country. These men and women work for us, to protect our country. They should be paid and not told to have a yard sale to make do.
> --_Sue, Loudoun County_
>
>
> * * *
>
> My husband has worked tirelessly as a member of the all-volunteer Coast Guard Auxiliary. He teaches Safe Boating classes and has used his boat as a vessel to assist in search and rescue in Sector Hampton Roads. Now with the shutdown, he cannot do any of the boat safety activities to help the Coast Guard keep our waters safe. It is ridiculous to shut down the federal government -- but it is dangerous to have curbed the lifesaving activities of the Coast Guard and their invaluable volunteer auxiliarists!
> --_Mary, Williamsburg_
>
>
> * * *
>
> My father works on the US Coast Guard base in Portsmouth, VA. Even though my father hasn't gotten a paycheck, Hampton University still wants the payments for my tuition. Just because his paychecks have stopped doesn't mean our bills have.
> --_Trinity, Suffolk_
>
>
> * * *
>
> My son is a Chief in the Coast Guard. For the past 19 years, he went to sea to protect our coastline, enforce our laws, and rescue those in need. Over the years he missed countless holidays, birthdays, and anniversaries with family and friends. Now, there's no respect for his sacrifices and service. This is disgraceful. This is un-American.
> --_Gary, Chesapeake_
>
>
> * * *
>
> My husband is a civilian employee of the Coast Guard. We are having to pull money from savings and significantly change our spending habits just to make sure we can make it through the month. We worry about paying our mortgage and keeping the heat and lights on. A wall will not help border security, and everyday working people are paying the price for a pointless standoff over it.
> --_Samantha, Herndon_

Samantha, Gary, Trinity, Mary, Sue, Lisa, and Katherine said it better than I can: Disgraceful. Unbelievable. Ridiculous. Dangerous. Un-American.

They wrote to me because they're hurting, they're worried, and they need someone to fight for them in Washington, D.C. And that's exactly what I'm going to do -- fight tooth and nail to reopen the government and give these hardworking people the paychecks they've been denied because of this reckless temper tantrum from the President.

Thanks for reading, and for fighting by my side.

_-Tim_

name: =?utf-8?Q?Bill=20Bryant?=
subject: 500 Words
body:
|
---
| |
---
| | Happy 2019!

Last April, at the suggestion of several supporters, I emailed my first “500 Words”, an update of what I’m up to and thinking about, and said I’d do it again if enough people wanted me to. Scores of you responded “yes”, so I sent a second one last October, and people asked me to keep ‘em coming. So, as long as you’re interested, I’ll send a few a year. Here’s No. 3.

You are receiving this because at some point you were identified as a supporter, someone who wants to keep in touch, or you asked to be added to the list. If you know somebody who would like to receive this, have them email me at [billbryantwa@gmail.com](mailto:billbryantwa@gmail.com). If you want to unsubscribe, please click the unsubscribe link at the bottom.
---
| |
## **WHAT I'VE BEEN DOING**
---
| In mid-October, friends and I hiked up to the Alpine Lakes. We hoped for autumn color, packed for a snowstorm, lucked out: crisp nights-sunny days. The climb can be steep, especially if you get a bit off-route, but once you’re up there: Wow, just Wow.
---
|
---
| In many countries, a place so special would be a helicopter-in village for the rich, but the Alpine Lakes belong to all of us. It is ours to enjoy, then pass on to the next generations. Many things combine to make America an exceptional nation, among them is our dedication to preserving spectacular places for “the betterment and enjoyment of all the people”. Our commitment to public lands is an expression of our democratic values.

Right before Thanksgiving, Barbara and I were honored to help USO volunteers serve a pre-Thanksgiving dinner to our troops (some who were being deployed that day). These people risk their lives protecting our democratic values. They deserve much more than a turkey dinner; they deserve our gratitude. If you’d like to help them, contact: [officemanager@usonw.org](https://twitter.us18.list-manage.com/track/click?u=c5ac11f23df6ef9dfb9e604c5&id=46c9ebe110&e=ef726ca89e)
---
|
---
| |
## **WHAT I'VE BEEN THINKING ABOUT**
---
| As a kid in Hoodsport, I passionately wanted to become an oceanographer. I didn’t, but my passion lives. That’s why since the late 1990s, I’ve worked to save Puget Sound, why I made that a priority while on the port commission, and why I served on the Puget Sound Partnership.

One whose passion was even stronger than mine, was Billy Frank Jr. I thought about Billy this past Thanksgiving, around the anniversary of him being posthumously honored with the Medal of Freedom.

I remembered how on our last real conversation (2011), we walked along the Nisqually River as Billy talked about how we cannot save salmon without saving Puget Sound, how we cannot save Puget Sound without saving our watersheds and deltas, and about how that requires sustainable forest management and responsible development.

That’s all on us, and we’re failing: habitat is shrinking; salmon are testing positive for cocaine and drugs; orca are starving.

Some propose throwing money at the problem, and while more money is needed, spending more doing more of what we’ve been doing might be enough to appear to be doing something, but it won’t get the job done. We need historic agreements among competing groups. We need massive shifts in how we dispose of waste and storm water. We need the kind of leadership Billy provided, the kind that looks out a few generations, not to the next election.
---
| |
## **WHAT I'VE READ**
---
| Two books, perfect for reading with scotch in hand in front of the fire:
---
| | [ ](https://twitter.us18.list-manage.com/track/click?u=c5ac11f23df6ef9dfb9e604c5&id=4476e35c9e&e=ef726ca89e)
---
Ivan Doig’s [_Winter Brothers_](https://twitter.us18.list-manage.com/track/click?u=c5ac11f23df6ef9dfb9e604c5&id=bd49b67ab7&e=ef726ca89e) takes you on a journey into 19th Century Olympic Peninsula, and the life of James Swan: oysterman, author, friend & schoolteacher to the Makaw, railroad booster, customs agent, secretary to congressional delegate Isaac Stevens, judge, Smithsonian collector, and thankfully, diarist.
---
| | [ ](https://twitter.us18.list-manage.com/track/click?u=c5ac11f23df6ef9dfb9e604c5&id=55347e92c4&e=ef726ca89e)
---

Melinda Mueller’s [_What The Ice_](https://twitter.us18.list-manage.com/track/click?u=c5ac11f23df6ef9dfb9e604c5&id=fa328cb692&e=ef726ca89e)[ _Gets_](https://twitter.us18.list-manage.com/track/click?u=c5ac11f23df6ef9dfb9e604c5&id=39ac36c15c&e=ef726ca89e) , is a poetic telling of the heroes and heroism that was the Shackleton Antarctic Expedition. Unbelievable story, magnificently written.
I read it twice.
---
| That’s No. 3, another 500 words will be sent out later this year. Keep in touch between editions of 500 words by liking my Facebook page: [@billbryantwashington](https://twitter.us18.list-manage.com/track/click?u=c5ac11f23df6ef9dfb9e604c5&id=1f96a413a3&e=ef726ca89e)

**\- Bill**
**Photos:**

2\. USO Pre-Thanksgiving
3\. Billy Frank Jr. (2011)_

name: Tina Smith
subject: Add your name: Protect low-wage contractors hurt by the shutdown
disclaimer_text: Paid for by Tina Smith for Minnesota
body:
I'm fighting to make sure they get the back pay they deserve.

| [](https://action.tinaforminnesota.com/page/m/4bc4d20f/c1f7015/61f1c559/1140c67e/277538285/VEsH/ "https://action.tinaforminnesota.com/page/s/rtw_201901_backpay?source=rtw_20190122_em-full") Friend, Low- and mid-wage federal contractors -- the people who provide security in our federal office buildings or clean up office spaces at night -- are too often invisible as it is. But during Donald Trump's reckless government shutdown, they aren't getting paid -- and they haven't received back pay after previous shutdowns. That is flat-out wrong. **That 's why I introduced a bill last week to make sure these contractors get the back pay they deserve this time. [Will you add your name now to join the fight? If 10,000 people sign on as citizen co-sponsors of this bill today, we'll send a clear message that these hardworking people must be treated fairly.](https://action.tinaforminnesota.com/page/m/4bc4d20f/c1f7015/61f1c559/1140c67e/277538285/VEsE/ "https://action.tinaforminnesota.com/page/s/rtw_201901_backpay?source=rtw_20190122_em-full")** [Add Your Name](https://action.tinaforminnesota.com/page/m/4bc4d20f/c1f7015/61f1c559/1140c67e/277538285/VEsF/ "https://action.tinaforminnesota.com/page/s/rtw_201901_backpay?source=rtw_20190122_em-full") With the shutdown now in day 32, many of these folks are having to make decisions about which bills to pay or which prescriptions to fill. We need to do everything we can to make sure these people are treated fairly and reimbursed for the wages they lost. **Friend, add your name now to protect low- and mid-wage federal contractors:[https://action.tinaforminnesota.com/backpay](https://action.tinaforminnesota.com/page/m/4bc4d20f/c1f7015/61f1c559/1140c67e/277538285/VEsC/ "https://action.tinaforminnesota.com/page/s/rtw_201901_backpay?source=rtw_20190122_em-full")** Fighting with you, Tina
